title: Newton's laws of motion — the three rules that govern all movement
examTip: Newton's Third Law pairs always act on DIFFERENT objects — if you say they cancel out, you'll lose the mark every time.
introTips: Newton's First Law sounds obvious but the logic catches people out — balanced forces do NOT mean no forces.
F = ma questions are straightforward if you identify which value is missing and rearrange before substituting.
Newton's Third Law questions often ask you to identify the 'reaction force' — name both objects and both directions.
intro: Everything that moves — or doesn't — obeys three rules discovered by Isaac Newton in the 1680s. Cars, rockets, your body when you slip on ice: all of it comes down to these three laws. Nail them and you've unlocked the core of mechanics.

keyPoints.heading: Newton's First Law — inertia and balanced forces
keyPoints.content: Newton's First Law: An object will remain at rest, or continue moving in a straight line at constant speed, unless acted upon by a resultant (net) force. Inertia is the tendency of objects to resist changes in their motion. A more massive object has greater inertia — it's harder to start moving, stop, or change direction. If the resultant force on an object is zero, there is no acceleration — the object either stays still or continues at the same velocity. This is NOT the same as saying there are no forces. A book sitting on a table has weight acting downward and a normal reaction force acting upward — both forces are real, but they balance so the resultant is zero and the book doesn't move. Real examples: a spaceship drifting far from any gravity experiences essentially no force → it continues at constant velocity indefinitely. A skater on frictionless ice (ignoring air resistance) would never slow down — friction and air resistance are the resultant forces that slow real objects.
writeBullets: Newton's First Law — object stays at rest or moves at constant velocity unless a resultant force acts
Inertia — tendency to resist changes in motion; greater mass = greater inertia
Resultant force = 0 → no acceleration (but forces may still be present and balanced)
Balanced forces ≠ no forces — they just cancel to give zero net force
Book on table: weight down balanced by normal reaction up → stays still
A spacecraft far from gravity: no net force → continues at constant velocity forever

keyTerms.def: The single force that has the same effect as all the individual forces acting on an object combined
The tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion; measured by its mass

keyPoints.memTip: Newton 1: No resultant force → no change in motion. Think 'coasting' — if nothing pushes or pulls you, you keep doing what you were doing.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The classic mistake is 'balanced forces mean the object is stationary' — remind Mabel that a car at constant speed on a motorway also has balanced forces.

keyPoints.heading: Newton's Second Law — F = ma
keyPoints.content: Newton's Second Law: The resultant force acting on an object is equal to its mass multiplied by its acceleration. F = ma. Unit of force: newton (N). 1N is the force needed to give a mass of 1kg an acceleration of 1 m/s². Rearrangements: a = F/m (more force → more acceleration; more mass → less acceleration). m = F/a. Worked example 1: a 1200kg car accelerates at 3 m/s². Resultant force = 1200 × 3 = 3600N. Worked example 2: a resultant force of 150N acts on a 50kg object. a = F/m = 150 ÷ 50 = 3 m/s². Worked example 3: a force of 20N produces an acceleration of 4 m/s². Mass = F/a = 20 ÷ 4 = 5kg. Inertial mass: the mass measured by F = ma is called inertial mass — it quantifies the object's resistance to acceleration. Experiments confirm inertial mass equals gravitational mass (the mass in W = mg) to extremely high precision.
writeBullets: F = ma: resultant force (N) = mass (kg) × acceleration (m/s²)
1 newton = force to accelerate 1kg by 1 m/s²
Rearrangements: a = F/m and m = F/a
Greater mass → smaller acceleration for the same force
Greater force → greater acceleration for the same mass
Inertial mass — measured from F = ma; equals gravitational mass in all experiments

keyTerms.def: Unit of force; 1N accelerates a 1kg mass at 1 m/s²
Mass measured by resistance to acceleration using F = ma; equal to gravitational mass

keyPoints.memTip: Force triangle: F on top, m and a below. Cover what you want to find: m×a = F, F÷m = a, F÷a = m.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Multi-step questions combine F = ma with kinematics (a = Δv/t) — practise linking the equations.

keyPoints.heading: Newton's Third Law — action and reaction pairs
keyPoints.content: Newton's Third Law: When object A exerts a force on object B, object B exerts an equal and opposite force on object A. These are called a Newton's Third Law pair (or action-reaction pair). Key properties: equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, same type of force, act on DIFFERENT objects. Because the forces act on different objects, they CANNOT cancel each other out — cancellation only occurs when forces act on the SAME object. Example 1: you push against a wall. You exert a force on the wall (you → wall, pushing right). The wall exerts an equal force back on you (wall → you, pushing left). That's why you don't fall forward — the reaction force acts on you. Example 2: a rocket expels gas downward at high speed. The engine exerts a force on the gas (downward). By Newton's Third Law, the gas exerts an equal force on the engine (upward) — this is the thrust that propels the rocket. Example 3: the Earth pulls you downward (gravity). You pull the Earth upward with exactly the same force — but because the Earth is 6 × 10²⁴ kg, your pull barely accelerates it at all.
writeBullets: Newton's Third Law — every action has an equal and opposite reaction on the OTHER object
Action-reaction pair: equal magnitude, opposite direction, same force type, different objects
Forces act on DIFFERENT objects — they cannot cancel each other
Wall example: you push wall → wall pushes you back with equal force
Rocket: engine pushes gas down → gas pushes rocket up (thrust)
Earth pulls you down → you pull Earth up with equal force (but Earth barely moves)

keyTerms.def: Two forces that are equal, opposite, same type, and act on two different interacting objects

keyPoints.memTip: Third Law pairs always come in sentences with two subjects: 'The [A] pulls/pushes the [B]' and 'The [B] pulls/pushes the [A] back.'
keyPoints.tutor_note: The most common exam error is saying Third Law pairs cancel — emphasise DIFFERENT OBJECTS every single lesson.

keyPoints.heading: Weight, mass and free body diagrams
keyPoints.content: Weight is the force of gravity acting on an object. Equation: W = mg. W in newtons (N), m in kilograms (kg), g = gravitational field strength in N/kg. On Earth's surface: g ≈ 9.8 N/kg (sometimes rounded to 10 N/kg in exam calculations — the question will say which to use). Mass is the amount of matter in an object — constant everywhere in the universe. Weight varies with location because g varies. Worked example: a 70kg person. On Earth: W = 70 × 9.8 = 686N. On the Moon (g ≈ 1.6 N/kg): W = 70 × 1.6 = 112N. In deep space (g ≈ 0): W ≈ 0N — but mass is still 70kg. Free body diagrams show all the forces acting on a single object as arrows from (or on) the object, with the arrow length proportional to the force size and the arrow direction showing the force direction. Labels should name the force and give its value if known. To find the resultant force, add forces in the same direction and subtract forces in opposite directions. If resultant ≠ 0, the object accelerates in the direction of the resultant force.
writeBullets: Weight W = mg (N); g = 9.8 N/kg on Earth's surface
Mass (kg) — amount of matter, constant everywhere
Weight (N) — gravitational force, varies with g at different locations
Moon: g ≈ 1.6 N/kg — objects weigh less but have the same mass
Free body diagram — arrows showing all forces on ONE object, length proportional to size
Resultant force = sum of forces; if non-zero, object accelerates in that direction

keyTerms.def: The gravitational force on an object; W = mg, measured in newtons
Force per unit mass at a location; on Earth g ≈ 9.8 N/kg
A diagram showing all forces acting on a single object as labelled arrows

keyPoints.memTip: Weight = mg. Mass stays, Weight wanders. Mass is constant, Weight changes with gravity.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often write weight in kg — it's always in newtons. Test this with quick unit-check questions.

tables.title: Newton's three laws — summary
rows: No resultant force → no change in motion (stays still or constant velocity)
Car at constant speed on motorway: driving force = friction + air resistance
Balanced forces means object is stationary — WRONG, it could be moving at constant velocity
F = ma: resultant force = mass × acceleration
F = ma uses the RESULTANT force, not just the applied force
Equal and opposite forces act on TWO DIFFERENT objects
Rocket pushes gas down; gas pushes rocket up
Third Law pairs cancel — WRONG, they act on different objects so cannot cancel

0.question: A car travels at constant speed on a level road. What can you say about the forces acting on it?
options: There are no forces acting on the car
The driving force is greater than friction
0.correctFeedback: Correct! Constant velocity means zero acceleration, which means zero resultant force — the forces are balanced.
0.wrongFeedback: Constant velocity means zero acceleration. By Newton's First Law, zero acceleration means zero resultant force — forces are balanced, not absent.

1.question: A resultant force of 600N acts on a 150kg object. What is its acceleration?
1.wrongFeedback: Use a = F/m (rearranged from F = ma). a = 600 ÷ 150 = 4 m/s².

2.question: You push a wall with a force of 50N. According to Newton's Third Law, what force does the wall exert on you?
options: More than 50N because the wall is heavier
2.correctFeedback: Correct! Newton's Third Law: equal and opposite force, back on you — 50N pushing you away from the wall.
2.wrongFeedback: Newton's Third Law: the wall exerts an equal (50N) and opposite force back on you. The forces are equal regardless of mass.

3.question: A 60kg person stands on the Moon where g = 1.6 N/kg. What is their weight on the Moon?
3.correctFeedback: Correct! W = mg = 60 × 1.6 = 96N. Mass stays at 60kg, weight changes with g.
3.wrongFeedback: Use W = mg. On the Moon: W = 60 × 1.6 = 96N. The person's mass (60kg) doesn't change, only their weight does.

commonMistakes: Saying Newton's Third Law force pairs cancel — they CANNOT cancel because they act on different objects.
Confusing mass and weight — mass is in kg and never changes; weight is in newtons and depends on gravity.
Using the applied force instead of the resultant force in F = ma — always calculate resultant first.
Saying 'balanced forces means the object is stationary' — it could be moving at constant velocity.
Giving weight in kg — always convert: weight = mg is in newtons, not kilograms.
examTips: When identifying a Newton's Third Law pair, write two sentences: '[A] exerts [force] on [B]' and '[B] exerts [force] on [A]' — name both objects explicitly.
For F = ma: always find the RESULTANT force first (driving force minus friction) before using the equation.
Free body diagram marks: arrows must have correct relative lengths, correct labels, and correct directions — check all three.
revisionCardBullets: Newton's First Law — no resultant force → no change in motion (rest or constant velocity)
Inertia — resistance to change in motion; greater mass = greater inertia
Balanced forces ≠ no forces — resultant is zero, but individual forces exist
Newton's Second Law — F = ma (N = kg × m/s²)
Rearrangements: a = F/m and m = F/a
Newton's Third Law — equal, opposite forces on TWO DIFFERENT objects
Third Law pairs do NOT cancel — different objects
W = mg; g ≈ 9.8 N/kg on Earth, ≈ 1.6 N/kg on Moon
Mass (kg) is constant everywhere; weight (N) varies with g
Free body diagrams — labelled arrows showing all forces on one object
Resultant force ≠ 0 → object accelerates in direction of resultant
